topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt harsh, exposing every flaw in the canvas. I stood before my latest series, the vibrant chaos I’d poured my soul into, now hanging on white walls that weren't mine. A cold knot tightened in my chest, squeezing the air from my lungs. I watched a patron circle the piece, nodding approvingly, their gaze lingering on the texture I’d painstakingly built. When they finally moved on, the silence that followed was deafening. I wanted to turn, to snatch the work back, but my feet felt rooted to the polished floor. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my temples. It was a profound sense of violation, like watching someone rearrange your private memories without consulting you.

[story 2]

I found the notification on my phone, a stark, unwelcome intrusion. My signature piece, the one I’d been preparing for months, was on display downtown. Curiosity warred with a sudden, sharp wave of dismay. When I arrived, the space felt sterile, the air thick with the scent of expensive perfume and uninvited admiration. Seeing my brushstrokes, my very expression captured in oil and pigment, hanging there, unshielded, sent a tremor through me. I walked slowly toward the artwork, my hands trembling slightly as I traced the outline of a particular shadow. A tightness bloomed low in my stomach, a heavy, dull ache that made my breath catch in my throat. I couldn't bring myself to speak to the curator; the words felt inadequate against the sheer audacity of the display.

[story 3]

The gallery opening was a blur of polite chatter and forced smiles. Then, I saw it. My abstract landscape, the one I’d wrestled into existence over a year, dominating a central wall. A wave of intense discomfort washed over me, leaving a strange hollowness behind my ribs. I watched as people pointed, their murmurs sounding like tiny, sharp stones against my ears. I retreated to a quiet corner, pulling my coat tighter around myself, trying to shrink into the shadows. My knuckles were white where I gripped my handbag strap. Every glance directed toward my work felt like an unwelcome scrutiny, a spotlight illuminating something intensely personal that I hadn't intended to share with this audience. The feeling settled deep within me, heavy and resistant to movement.
